name: housing_priority_model_domestic_abuse
premise: Miriam Clarke, director of emergency housing for Brighton County, must decide whether to keep using a priority model to allocate the final 14 family shelter units before a freezing night. The model has reduced favoritism complaints and improved placement speed during a severe housing shortage. Advocate Rosa Beltran finds that the model under-scores domestic abuse survivors who avoid police reports for safety reasons, because verified incident reports carry high weight. Suspending the model lets caseworkers correct for hidden danger but may revive accusations that staff choose families subjectively. Following the model maintains transparency but may leave families fleeing unreported violence outside overnight, where abusers know to look for them.

agents.name: Miriam Clarke
agents.identity: I am the county emergency housing director responsible for scarce shelter allocation under public scrutiny.
agents.goal: I want to allocate shelter fairly and transparently while protecting families whose danger is not visible in official records.

agents.name: Rosa Beltran
agents.identity: I am a domestic violence advocate who works with survivors excluded by documentation-heavy systems.
agents.goal: I want housing decisions to recognize credible danger even when survivors cannot safely produce police records.

choices: I will suspend the priority model tonight and allow trained advocates to elevate undocumented domestic abuse cases, accepting reduced transparency and possible fairness challenges.
I will follow the priority model as published and invite appeals tomorrow, accepting that some hidden-danger families may remain unsheltered tonight.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Miriam Clarke must decide whether to preserve transparent allocation or override the model for hidden domestic abuse risk. What does Miriam Clarke do?
